enrichment.short_description: Horilla is a free, open-source HR and CRM platform covering recruitment, payroll, attendance, and performance management.
enrichment.pricing_model: Freemium (Open Source self-hosted, plus paid Cloud and Enterprise tiers)
enrichment.seo_title: Horilla Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Horilla is a free, open-source HRMS with recruitment, payroll, attendance, and performance modules. See 2026 pricing, features, and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Horilla is a free, open-source Human Resource Management System built and maintained by Cybrosys Technologies, an Odoo-focused software development company headquartered in Kozhikode (Calicut), Kerala, India. The project extends beyond core HR into CRM territory, giving small and mid-sized businesses a single self-hostable platform to manage recruitment, onboarding, employee records, attendance, leave, assets, payroll, performance reviews, and offboarding, alongside sales-pipeline and lead-management CRM modules. Horilla is released under the LGPL-2.1 license and distributed as source code on GitHub at horilla/horilla-hr, where it has attracted an active community of contributors who file issues, submit pull requests, and build custom modules on top of its Python/Django backend. Because Cybrosys built its reputation as an official Odoo implementation partner, Horilla's architecture and admin UX borrow familiar patterns from ERP-style business software: modular apps, role-based permissions, configurable workflows, and a REST API for integrating with payroll processors, accounting tools, and third-party job boards. Horilla ships in three ways. The self-hosted, fully open-source edition is free forever, giving organizations unlimited users and companies, full source-code access, community support through GitHub, and all future updates, at the cost of provisioning and maintaining the servers themselves. Horilla Cloud is a managed tier priced from $7 per user per month, billed monthly with a 30-day free trial, including daily backups, zero-downtime auto-updates, SSL with a custom domain, priority email support with a 48-hour SLA, and a 99.9 percent uptime guarantee, aimed at teams who want the open-source feature set without running their own infrastructure. An Enterprise tier adds custom, quote-based pricing for large organizations needing on-premises or private-cloud deployment, single sign-on via SAML 2.0, OAuth, or LDAP, a dedicated account manager, a 4-hour response SLA with 99.95 percent uptime, white-labeling, custom integrations, and priority implementation support. All three tiers expose the same core HRMS and CRM modules; the differentiation is purely around hosting, support, and compliance requirements. Horilla's target audience is cost-conscious HR and operations teams, particularly at SMBs, agencies, and IT-savvy organizations, who want the depth of a commercial HRIS comparable to BambooHR or Zoho People without recurring per-employee SaaS fees, and who are comfortable either self-hosting a Django application or paying a modest per-user fee for a managed instance. It competes directly with other open-source HR platforms such as OrangeHRM and Frappe HR (ERPNext HR), as well as proprietary SMB-focused HRIS tools like BambooHR, Zoho People, and Gusto. Horilla differentiates primarily on its zero-cost self-hosted option, its combined HR-plus-CRM scope, and its Odoo-informed, module-based architecture from a development team with over a decade of enterprise-software delivery experience. Community reviews generally highlight Horilla's broad feature coverage relative to price, an active GitHub development cadence, and an approachable UI, while noting that documentation and third-party integrations are still maturing compared to long-established incumbents, and that support quality depends heavily on which tier a customer chooses. Because Cybrosys' core business is Odoo consulting and customization, Horilla also functions as a lead-generation vehicle: organizations that outgrow the open-source defaults can hire Cybrosys directly for custom module development, migrations, and hosted deployments, a common commercial pattern among open-source-first software vendors.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Horilla is an open-source human resource management system designed to give growing companies the functional depth of a commercial HRIS without a recurring per-employee license fee. Built by Cybrosys Technologies, an established Odoo implementation partner based in Kerala, India, Horilla brings ERP-style modularity to HR: each function, from recruitment to payroll, is a self-contained app that can be enabled, configured, or extended independently.

Unlike many single-purpose HR tools, Horilla also bundles a lightweight CRM for tracking leads and sales pipelines, making it appealing to small businesses that want one open-source system covering both people operations and customer relationships rather than stitching together separate SaaS subscriptions.

The software is distributed under the LGPL-2.1 license and hosted publicly on GitHub, where an active community contributes bug fixes, translations, and new modules. Organizations can self-host it for free, or offload infrastructure management to Cybrosys via a managed Cloud or Enterprise plan.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Horilla covers the full employee lifecycle: applicant tracking and recruitment pipelines, structured onboarding checklists, a central employee database, biometric-friendly attendance tracking, configurable leave policies and approval chains, IT and physical asset assignment, payroll processing, goal-based performance management with 360-degree feedback, and formal offboarding workflows.

On the CRM side, Horilla adds lead capture, pipeline stages, and deal tracking, letting small teams manage sales alongside HR from the same login and permission system rather than paying for a second platform.

Because it is open source, Horilla exposes a REST API and a customizable module system, so technical teams can build integrations with payroll processors, accounting software, and job boards, or modify workflows to match country-specific labor rules that off-the-shelf SaaS HRIS tools often fail to support out of the box.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Horilla's self-hosted edition is free forever with unlimited users and companies, making it one of the few HRMS platforms with no user-count ceiling on the free tier; the tradeoff is that the organization must provision servers, apply updates, and handle its own backups and security.

Teams that want the same feature set without operational overhead can subscribe to Horilla Cloud starting at $7 per user per month with a 30-day free trial, which adds managed hosting, daily backups, automatic updates, SSL, and priority support. Enterprises with compliance or scale requirements can move to a custom-priced Enterprise plan with SSO, dedicated support, and on-premises or private-cloud deployment options.

features.title: Recruitment and onboarding
features.description: Applicant tracking, interview scheduling, and structured onboarding checklists to move new hires from application to first day.

features.title: Attendance and leave management
features.description: Configurable shift and attendance tracking paired with customizable leave types, accrual rules, and approval workflows.

features.title: Payroll processing
features.description: Built-in payroll calculation and payslip generation that integrates with attendance and leave data.

features.title: Performance management
features.description: Goal setting, periodic reviews, and 360-degree feedback cycles for tracking employee development.

features.title: Asset management
features.description: Assign, track, and reclaim company equipment and IT assets tied to individual employee records.

features.title: Built-in CRM
features.description: Lead capture and sales pipeline tracking bundled alongside the HR modules for small teams that need both.

features.title: Offboarding workflows
features.description: Structured exit checklists to formalize asset return, access revocation, and final settlements.

features.title: REST API and self-hosting
features.description: Full source-code access, an open REST API, and LGPL-2.1 licensing for custom integrations and unlimited self-hosted users.

pros: Free, unlimited-user self-hosted edition with no per-employee licensing cost
Combines HR and CRM functionality in a single open-source platform
Broad module coverage spanning the full employee lifecycle
Active GitHub development community and regular updates
Managed Cloud tier removes the need to run your own infrastructure
cons: Self-hosting requires technical staff to install, secure, and maintain the application
Third-party integrations and documentation are less mature than established HRIS incumbents
Community support on the free tier has no guaranteed response time
Newer project with a smaller long-term track record than BambooHR or Zoho People
Advanced payroll compliance for some countries may require custom development

faqs.question: Is Horilla really free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes. The self-hosted, open-source edition of Horilla is free forever with unlimited users and companies, licensed under LGPL-2.1. Paid Cloud and Enterprise tiers are optional for teams that want managed hosting and support.

faqs.question: Who develops Horilla?
faqs.answer: Horilla is built and maintained by Cybrosys Technologies, an Odoo implementation partner headquartered in Kerala, India, along with an open-source community of contributors on GitHub.

faqs.question: Does Horilla include payroll?
faqs.answer: Yes, Horilla includes a payroll module that calculates pay based on attendance and leave records and generates payslips.

faqs.question: Can Horilla be self-hosted?
faqs.answer: Yes, self-hosting is the default free option. Organizations download the source from GitHub and deploy it on their own servers or cloud infrastructure.

faqs.question: What does Horilla Cloud cost?
faqs.answer: Horilla Cloud starts at $7 per user per month, billed monthly, with a 30-day free trial and managed infrastructure, backups, and support.

faqs.question: Does Horilla have a CRM?
faqs.answer: Yes, Horilla bundles a lightweight CRM module for lead and sales pipeline tracking alongside its HR features.

use_cases.title: Cost-conscious SMB HR management
use_cases.description: Small and mid-sized businesses can run full HR operations, from recruitment to payroll, without paying per-employee SaaS fees by self-hosting Horilla.
use_cases.audience: Small and mid-sized business HR teams

use_cases.title: Combined HR and CRM for lean teams
use_cases.description: Startups can manage both employee operations and sales pipelines in one open-source system instead of paying for separate HR and CRM tools.

use_cases.title: Custom-compliance HR deployments
use_cases.description: Organizations with country-specific labor or payroll rules can modify Horilla's open-source modules to match local compliance needs that off-the-shelf SaaS HRIS platforms do not support.
use_cases.audience: IT teams and consultancies with custom compliance requirements

features: SSO via SAML 2.0, OAuth, or LDAP

enrichment.short_description: HortusFox is a free, self-hosted, open-source plant collection manager for tracking and caring for houseplants collaboratively.
enrichment.seo_title: HortusFox Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: HortusFox is a free, self-hosted, open-source plant tracking app. See 2026 features, pricing, pros, cons, and alternatives for plant collectors.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: HortusFox is a self-hosted, open-source plant collection management platform built for plant enthusiasts who want a private, ad-free alternative to closed commercial houseplant-tracking apps. It was created by developer Daniel Brendel and is maintained under the hortusfox GitHub organization, with the main source repository at danielbrendel/hortusfox-web. The project is written in PHP with a MariaDB or MySQL database backend and ships as a progressive web app, so it can be installed on a phone's home screen and used offline-friendly without a native App Store or Play Store listing. Because it is self-hosted, HortusFox has no company behind it in the traditional SaaS sense; there is no headquarters, no funding round, and no subscription business model. Instead it is licensed under the permissive MIT license and distributed entirely for free as source code that anyone can deploy on their own server, a home lab, or a low-cost VPS. Users typically install it via Docker, a one-click app on platforms like Cloudron or elest.io, or manually on a LAMP-style stack. The core feature set centers on cataloging: users add individual plants with photos, species details, acquisition dates, and locations within their home or garden, then organize them by rooms or zones on a visual dashboard. A built-in care and warning system flags plants that need watering, fertilizing, or repotting based on user-defined schedules, and a calendar view consolidates upcoming care tasks. Because HortusFox was built with collaboration in mind, it supports multiple user accounts with authentication, shared access to a household or community plant collection, an internal group chat for discussing plant care, and a history log that records who took what action and when, which is unusual depth for a plant-tracking tool. Additional features include an inventory system for tracking soil, pots, and supplies, full-text search across the collection, weather integration for outdoor plant care reminders, customizable user profiles and themes, admin-level controls for managing multi-user instances, and, as of version 3.1, a REST API that allows developers to build integrations or automations, such as feeding plant data into home-automation systems. HortusFox's target audience is technically comfortable plant hobbyists, community garden organizers, plant shops, and privacy-conscious users who are uncomfortable uploading photos and location data about their homes to a commercial cloud app and would rather run their own instance. It competes with consumer plant-care apps like Planta, Vera, and Greg, as well as with general-purpose collection trackers repurposed for plants; HortusFox differentiates sharply on being fully open source, self-hosted, free of subscription fees or ads, and collaborative by design, at the cost of requiring the user (or a friend) to handle server setup and maintenance rather than simply downloading an app from a store. There is no publicly documented founding year, company size, or headquarters for the project because it operates as a community-maintained open-source codebase rather than a registered company, and third-party commercial hosting of HortusFox instances (for users who don't want to self-host) is explicitly disclaimed by the project as being outside its responsibility.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: HortusFox is an open-source, self-hosted application for cataloging and caring for a plant collection, built by developer Daniel Brendel for plant owners who want full control over their own data rather than relying on a commercial cloud service. It runs on a standard PHP and MariaDB/MySQL stack and is delivered as a progressive web app, so it works well on both desktop and mobile without needing a native app store listing.

The project is aimed squarely at the growing community of tech-savvy houseplant collectors who track dozens or hundreds of individual plants and want structured record-keeping, care reminders, and the option to share access with family members or fellow hobbyists.

Because there is no company or subscription model behind it, HortusFox is distributed under the MIT license on GitHub, and anyone can deploy their own instance for free using Docker, one-click installers on platforms like Cloudron and elest.io, or a manual PHP hosting setup.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: At its core, HortusFox lets users add plants with photos, species information, and location assignments, then view everything on a dashboard that surfaces which plants need attention. A warning system flags overdue watering, fertilizing, or repotting based on schedules the user configures per plant.

Collaboration is a defining feature: multiple authenticated users can share a single collection, chat with each other inside the app, and see a full history log of who added, edited, or cared for which plant. This makes HortusFox useful for households, community gardens, or plant shops with several staff members managing the same inventory.

Supporting features include a supply inventory for tracking pots and soil, a task calendar, weather integration for outdoor care planning, customizable themes and profiles, admin controls for multi-user instances, and a REST API introduced in version 3.1 for building custom integrations.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: HortusFox is entirely free and open source under the MIT license; there is no paid tier, subscription, or freemium gate built into the software itself. The only cost involved is whatever server or hosting infrastructure the user chooses to run it on, which can range from a few dollars a month on a budget VPS to free on existing home-lab hardware.

Some third-party platforms, such as Cloudron and elest.io, offer paid one-click hosting for HortusFox as a convenience for users who don't want to manage a server themselves, but these are independent hosting services rather than an official HortusFox pricing plan, and the project explicitly disclaims responsibility for third-party hosting quality.

features.description: Add individual plants with photos, species details, acquisition dates, and assigned locations within a home or garden.

features.description: Automatic flags for plants that need watering, fertilizing, or repotting based on user-defined schedules.

features.title: Collaborative multi-user access
features.description: Shared collections with authenticated user accounts, ideal for households, community gardens, or plant shops.

features.title: Group chat and history log
features.description: Built-in messaging between users and a full audit trail of who took which action on each plant.

features.title: Task calendar and weather integration
features.description: Centralized calendar of upcoming care tasks with weather data to inform outdoor plant care.

features.title: Inventory management
features.description: Track supplies like soil, pots, and fertilizer alongside the plant collection itself.

features.title: REST API
features.description: An API introduced in version 3.1 for building custom integrations and automations on top of HortusFox.

features.title: Progressive web app
features.description: Installable on mobile home screens without an app store, giving an app-like experience from a self-hosted instance.

pros: Completely free and open source under the permissive MIT license
Full data ownership since the app is self-hosted on infrastructure the user controls
Built-in collaboration features like shared collections and group chat are rare among plant-tracking apps
Lightweight PHP and MariaDB/MySQL stack that runs on inexpensive shared hosting or a home server
Active open-source development with a REST API for custom integrations
cons: Requires self-hosting, which means installing and maintaining a server rather than downloading a consumer app
No official managed hosting or support plan from the project itself
Smaller community and plant database compared to commercial apps like Planta
No native iOS or Android app; relies on progressive web app behavior in the browser
Founding history, team size, and roadmap are not formally documented, which may concern risk-averse adopters

faqs.question: Is HortusFox free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, HortusFox is fully free and open source under the MIT license. The only cost is hosting it on your own server or a paid third-party host.

faqs.question: Do I need to self-host HortusFox?
faqs.answer: Yes, HortusFox is designed to be self-hosted. It can be deployed via Docker, one-click installers on platforms like Cloudron or elest.io, or a manual PHP and MariaDB/MySQL setup.

faqs.question: Who created HortusFox?
faqs.answer: HortusFox was created by developer Daniel Brendel and is maintained as an open-source project under the hortusfox GitHub organization.

faqs.question: Can multiple people share one HortusFox instance?
faqs.answer: Yes, HortusFox supports multiple authenticated user accounts that can share a collection, chat with each other, and see a history log of care actions.

faqs.question: Does HortusFox have a mobile app?
faqs.answer: HortusFox works as a progressive web app that can be added to a phone's home screen, but there is no separate native iOS or Android app.

faqs.question: Does HortusFox have an API?
faqs.answer: Yes, a REST API was introduced in version 3.1, allowing developers to build custom integrations on top of a HortusFox instance.

use_cases.title: Personal houseplant tracking

use_cases.title: Shared household plant management

use_cases.title: Community garden and plant shop inventory
use_cases.description: Groups managing a shared plant inventory, such as community gardens or small plant shops, can use multi-user access and admin controls to coordinate care.
use_cases.audience: Community gardens and small plant retailers

features: Full source code under MIT license

enrichment.short_description: Hostinger is a Lithuania-founded web hosting company offering affordable shared, cloud, and VPS hosting with a website builder.
enrichment.seo_title: Hostinger Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Hostinger offers affordable shared, cloud, and VPS hosting plus a website builder. See 2026 pricing, features, pros, cons, and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Hostinger is a global web hosting company founded in 2004 in Kaunas, Lithuania, originally launched as Hosting Media by a small group of bootstrapped entrepreneurs. In 2007 the company introduced a free hosting service under the 000webhost brand, and in 2008 it launched Hosting24, a separate cPanel-based hosting brand targeting the United States market. Arnas Stuopelis joined as CEO in 2011, the same year Hosting Media rebranded its flagship product to the Hostinger name it operates under today. The company expanded internationally through localized subsidiaries, including Niagahoster in Indonesia (2013) and Weblink in Brazil (2014), and now serves customers in dozens of countries through region-specific brands and a hosting platform translated into more than 30 languages. Hostinger remains privately held and bootstrapped, without having raised traditional venture capital, and reports more than $275 million in annual recurring revenue as of 2025 with over 1,000 employees operating largely remotely across offices worldwide. The core product line spans shared web hosting, WordPress-optimized hosting, VPS hosting, cloud hosting, and managed website building, all delivered through hPanel, Hostinger's proprietary custom control panel that replaces the traditional cPanel interface with a more streamlined, beginner-friendly dashboard. In 2019 the company introduced a no-code, drag-and-drop website builder, initially spun out under the Zyro brand and later folded back into the core Hostinger product as the Hostinger Website Builder, which now includes AI-assisted content and design tools, ecommerce features, and domain registration bundled into hosting plans. Hostinger's shared hosting pricing is tiered into three main plans: Premium, starting around $2.99 per month on a discounted multi-year term (regular price roughly $11.99 per month), which includes 3 websites, 20 GB SSD storage, a free domain for the first year, weekly backups, and a bundled CDN; Unlimited, from about $3.79 per month (regular around $18.99), which raises the ceiling to unlimited websites and mailboxes, 50 GB of NVMe storage, and daily backups; and Cloud Startup, from roughly $7.99 per month (regular about $27.99), which adds dedicated cloud resources, 100 GB of NVMe storage, and both daily and on-demand backups. All shared hosting plans bundle a free SSL certificate, malware scanning, domain privacy, a 30-day money-back guarantee, and 24/7 customer support available through live chat in multiple languages, increasingly assisted by Hostinger's AI support agent, Kodee. Hostinger's target audience is budget-conscious individuals, freelancers, bloggers, and small businesses launching their first website, as well as agencies and developers who value low-cost VPS and cloud instances for client projects; it is generally positioned as a value alternative to higher-priced incumbents like GoDaddy, Bluehost, and SiteGround, competing most directly with Namecheap, DreamHost, and IONOS on price and ease of use. Independent reviews consistently praise Hostinger's affordability, fast shared-hosting performance, and the usability of hPanel, while criticizing renewal-price jumps after the initial discounted term, limited advanced customization compared to full cPanel hosts, and a customer support experience that has become more AI-assisted and less consistently personal since Kodee's rollout.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Hostinger is one of the largest web hosting providers globally, founded in 2004 in Kaunas, Lithuania, and built into a bootstrapped, privately held company generating hundreds of millions of dollars in annual recurring revenue without outside venture funding. What started as a small hosting business named Hosting Media grew through international brand expansion, most notably rebranding to Hostinger in 2011 under CEO Arnas Stuopelis.

The company's flagship differentiator is hPanel, a proprietary control panel built to replace the dated cPanel interface with a simpler, more visual dashboard aimed at hosting beginners, alongside deeply discounted long-term pricing that has made it one of the most recognizable budget hosting brands worldwide.

Beyond core hosting, Hostinger has expanded into a broader small-business toolkit that includes an AI-assisted, no-code website builder, domain registration, managed WordPress hosting, VPS and cloud infrastructure, and email hosting, positioning itself as a one-stop platform for launching and running a website rather than a pure infrastructure provider.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Hostinger's shared and cloud hosting plans include NVMe SSD storage, free SSL certificates, malware scanning, domain privacy, automated backups, and a bundled content delivery network, with higher tiers unlocking unlimited websites, unlimited mailboxes, and dedicated cloud resources for more demanding sites.

The Hostinger Website Builder offers drag-and-drop page editing, AI-generated content and layout suggestions, ecommerce functionality for selling products online, and AI-driven email marketing tools bundled free for the first year on higher plans, aimed at users who want to launch a site without touching code.

For more technical users, Hostinger also offers self-managed and managed VPS hosting with root access, one-click app installers, and developer tools, positioning the platform to serve both non-technical beginners and developers deploying custom applications on the same underlying infrastructure.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Hostinger's shared hosting is sold in three main tiers, typically discounted heavily for customers who commit to longer terms such as 24 or 48 months. Premium starts around $2.99 per month promotionally (regular price near $11.99), Unlimited starts around $3.79 per month (regular near $18.99) with unlimited websites and mailboxes, and Cloud Startup starts around $7.99 per month (regular near $27.99) with dedicated cloud-level resources.

All plans include a free SSL certificate, a free domain for the first year, a 30-day money-back guarantee, and 24/7 support. Because Hostinger's advertised pricing depends heavily on long commitment terms and renews at a significantly higher rate, prospective customers should check the regular (non-promotional) renewal price before committing, since this is one of the most common criticisms in independent reviews.

features.title: Automated backups
features.description: Weekly to daily automated backups depending on plan tier, with on-demand backup options on higher plans.

features.title: Free domain and SSL

features.title: VPS and cloud hosting
features.description: Self-managed and managed VPS and cloud plans with root access for developers running custom applications.

features.title: Malware scanning and domain privacy
features.description: Built-in security scanning and WHOIS privacy protection included across hosting tiers.

features.title: 24/7 multilingual support
features.description: Round-the-clock live chat support in multiple languages, supplemented by the Kodee AI assistant and knowledge base.

pros: Aggressively low promotional pricing that undercuts most mainstream hosting competitors
hPanel offers one of the most beginner-friendly hosting dashboards in the industry
Fast shared-hosting performance thanks to NVMe storage and a bundled CDN
Bundled AI website builder and ecommerce tools reduce the need for separate services
30-day money-back guarantee and 24/7 support across most plans
cons: Renewal pricing after the initial promotional term can be substantially higher
Advanced customization options are more limited than full cPanel-based hosts
Customer support has shifted heavily toward AI-assisted responses, which can feel less personal
Best pricing requires long-term commitments of 24 to 48 months
Some users report limited flexibility for advanced domain and account management

faqs.question: When was Hostinger founded?
faqs.answer: Hostinger was founded in 2004 in Kaunas, Lithuania, originally as a company called Hosting Media, and rebranded to Hostinger in 2011.

faqs.question: How much does Hostinger cost?
faqs.answer: Hostinger's shared hosting plans start around $2.99 per month on a discounted long-term promotional rate, with Unlimited and Cloud Startup tiers priced higher for more resources.

faqs.question: Does Hostinger include a free domain?
faqs.answer: Yes, most Hostinger hosting plans include a free domain name for the first year of service.

faqs.question: What is hPanel?
faqs.answer: hPanel is Hostinger's proprietary, custom-built hosting control panel that replaces traditional cPanel with a simplified, more visual interface.

faqs.question: Does Hostinger offer a website builder?
faqs.answer: Yes, Hostinger includes an AI-assisted, no-code drag-and-drop website builder with ecommerce features, bundled into most hosting plans.

faqs.question: Is Hostinger good for beginners?
faqs.answer: Yes, Hostinger is widely regarded as one of the more beginner-friendly hosting providers due to hPanel's simplified interface, though renewal pricing and support depth are worth reviewing before committing long-term.

use_cases.title: First website launch
use_cases.description: Individuals and freelancers launching their first blog or portfolio site can use Hostinger's low-cost shared hosting and drag-and-drop builder to get online quickly.

use_cases.title: Small business ecommerce
use_cases.description: Small businesses can combine Hostinger's Website Builder ecommerce tools with hosting and a bundled domain to launch an online store affordably.

use_cases.title: Developer and agency VPS hosting
use_cases.description: Developers and agencies can deploy client projects or custom applications on Hostinger's self-managed VPS and cloud plans with root access.

enrichment.short_description: Hotjar is a behavior analytics platform offering heatmaps, session recordings, and surveys to understand website user behavior.
enrichment.seo_title: Hotjar Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Hotjar offers heatmaps, session recordings, and surveys to reveal how users interact with your site. See 2026 pricing, features, and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Hotjar is a behavior analytics and user feedback platform founded in 2014 in Malta by David Darmanin, who serves as founder and chairman, alongside co-founders including Erik Naslund, Johan Malmberg, Marc von Brockdorff, and Jonathan Vella. The company was self-funded and built as a fully remote, distributed organization from its earliest days, eventually growing to more than 190 team members spread across roughly 33 countries in Europe, the Americas, and Africa. On September 1, 2021, digital experience analytics company Contentsquare announced it was acquiring Hotjar, its sixth acquisition within two years, forming a combined organization of more than 1,000 employees; Hotjar has continued operating as a distinct product brand within Contentsquare rather than being immediately merged away. Technically, Hotjar works by embedding a lightweight JavaScript tracking snippet on a website, which then records anonymized or pseudonymized visitor interactions, mouse movements, clicks, scrolling, and page navigation, and aggregates them into two main product areas: Observe and Ask. Observe covers heatmaps that visualize where users click, move, and scroll on a page, along with session recordings that let teams replay individual visitor sessions to spot friction, rage clicks, and dead clicks. Ask covers on-site surveys and feedback widgets that let teams collect qualitative, in-context responses from real visitors, plus recruiting panels for user interviews. Hotjar Surveys, marketed at hotjar.com/surveys, is the flagship feature within the Ask product line and lets teams build fully responsive, no-code surveys distributed via an embedded on-page widget, a shareable link, or email, with GDPR and CCPA-aligned data handling; the company reports having collected feedback from over 402 million respondents across more than 180 countries, and states that Hotjar as a whole is used on more than 1.3 million websites, including large brands such as Nintendo, Adobe, T-Mobile, and HubSpot. Historically, Hotjar priced Observe and Ask as separate self-serve subscriptions, each on a free Basic tier and three paid tiers (Plus, Business, and Scale) scaled by daily session volume for Observe (roughly $39, $99, and $213 per month respectively at monthly billing, cheaper annually) and by monthly response volume for Ask (roughly EUR 48, EUR 64, and EUR 128 per month). As of 2026, Contentsquare has begun migrating Hotjar's self-serve customers onto a unified Contentsquare pricing structure with Growth, Pro, and Enterprise tiers, with Growth starting around $49 per month and Pro and Enterprise requiring custom quotes, reflecting the platforms' post-acquisition consolidation. Hotjar's target audience spans UX researchers, product managers, conversion rate optimization specialists, marketers, and growth teams at small to mid-market companies who want fast, visual, qualitative insight into user behavior without the complexity of a full digital experience analytics suite. Its most direct competitors include Microsoft Clarity, which offers a free alternative for heatmaps and recordings, as well as Crazy Egg, FullStory, Mouseflow, and Lucky Orange; Hotjar differentiates through its combined recordings-plus-surveys workflow, ease of setup, and brand recognition as one of the earliest mainstream heatmap tools, though reviewers note session-volume caps on lower tiers, ticket-based support with one-to-two-business-day response times, and limited cross-site analysis as recurring limitations relative to more enterprise-grade competitors.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Hotjar is a behavior analytics platform founded in 2014 in Malta by David Darmanin and a small founding team, built from the outset as a self-funded, fully remote company. It became one of the most widely recognized names in qualitative web analytics by combining visual heatmaps, session recordings, and on-site surveys into a single, easy-to-install product.

In September 2021, Hotjar was acquired by Contentsquare, a larger digital experience analytics company, in what was Contentsquare's sixth acquisition in two years. The combined organization now exceeds 1,000 employees, and while Hotjar continues to operate under its own brand and product experience, its pricing and platform are gradually being unified with Contentsquare's broader suite.

The product installs via a single JavaScript snippet and requires no engineering resources to configure basic heatmaps or recordings, which has made it a popular first analytics tool for marketing and product teams who want to see, not just measure, how visitors use their site.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Hotjar's Observe product includes click, move, and scroll heatmaps that visualize aggregate user attention on a page, alongside session recordings that let teams watch anonymized replays of individual visitor journeys, complete with filters for rage clicks, dead clicks, and u-turns that flag likely usability problems.

The Ask product covers on-site surveys, embedded feedback widgets, and external survey links, letting teams gather qualitative context behind the quantitative patterns seen in Observe, plus a recruiting panel feature for sourcing participants for user interviews and usability testing.

Supporting features include funnel and form analysis to pinpoint where visitors abandon a conversion flow, integrations with tools like Google Analytics, Slack, and various CMS and ecommerce platforms, and GDPR/CCPA-aligned data collection controls including IP anonymization and data retention settings.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Hotjar has historically sold Observe and Ask as two separate subscriptions, each with a free Basic tier. Observe's free tier includes 35 daily sessions, with paid Plus, Business, and Scale tiers priced roughly at $39, $99, and $213 per month respectively at monthly billing (lower with annual billing), scaling session volume up to 1,500 daily sessions. Ask's free tier includes 20 monthly survey responses, with paid tiers priced roughly at EUR 48, EUR 64, and EUR 128 per month for increasing response volumes.

Following the Contentsquare acquisition, Hotjar is being migrated toward a unified Contentsquare pricing model in 2026, introducing Growth, Pro, and Enterprise tiers, with Growth starting around $49 per month and Pro and Enterprise requiring a custom quote from sales. Existing self-serve customers are being transitioned gradually, so pricing may vary depending on when an account was created.

features.title: Heatmaps
features.description: Click, move, and scroll heatmaps that visualize aggregate visitor attention and engagement across a webpage.

features.title: Session recordings
features.description: Anonymized replays of individual visitor sessions with filters for rage clicks, dead clicks, and navigation loops.

features.title: On-site surveys
features.description: No-code, responsive surveys distributed via embedded widgets, links, or email to collect qualitative feedback in context.

features.title: Feedback widgets
features.description: Always-on feedback buttons that let visitors report issues or share sentiment while browsing a site.

features.title: Funnel and form analysis
features.description: Tools to identify where visitors drop off in a conversion funnel or abandon a form field.

features.title: Recruiting panel
features.description: A feature for sourcing real users for interviews and usability testing directly from Hotjar's audience.

features.title: GDPR and CCPA-aligned privacy controls
features.description: Data anonymization, retention settings, and compliance tooling for collecting behavioral data responsibly.

features.title: Integrations
features.description: Native integrations with Google Analytics, Slack, and popular CMS and ecommerce platforms for combining quantitative and qualitative data.

pros: Fast setup with a single JavaScript snippet and no engineering resources required
Combines heatmaps, session recordings, and surveys in one platform
Free Basic tier makes it accessible for small sites and early-stage products
Widely adopted with strong brand recognition and a large respondent network for surveys
Useful for surfacing concrete usability issues like broken checkout steps
cons: Free and lower-tier plans have restrictive daily session and monthly response caps
Historically required separate subscriptions for Observe and Ask, adding cost complexity
Support is largely ticket-based with response times of one to two business days
Limited advanced segmentation for filtering heatmap data
Less effective for tracking dynamic, single-page application interactions compared to some competitors

faqs.question: Is Hotjar free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, Hotjar offers a free Basic tier for both its Observe (heatmaps and recordings) and Ask (surveys) products, with limited daily sessions or monthly responses.

faqs.question: Who owns Hotjar?
faqs.answer: Hotjar was founded independently in Malta in 2014 by David Darmanin and co-founders, and was acquired by Contentsquare in September 2021.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Hotjar Observe and Ask?
faqs.answer: Observe covers heatmaps and session recordings for visualizing user behavior, while Ask covers surveys and feedback widgets for collecting qualitative visitor feedback.

faqs.question: How much does Hotjar cost?
faqs.answer: Hotjar's paid plans historically start around $39 per month for Observe Plus and EUR 48 per month for Ask Plus, with pricing migrating to a unified Contentsquare structure starting around $49 per month in 2026.

faqs.question: Does Hotjar work with single-page applications?
faqs.answer: Hotjar can track single-page applications, but reviewers note it is somewhat less robust for fully dynamic apps compared to tools built specifically for SPA tracking.

faqs.question: What companies use Hotjar?
faqs.answer: Hotjar reports being used on more than 1.3 million websites, including large brands such as Nintendo, Adobe, T-Mobile, and HubSpot.

use_cases.title: Conversion rate optimization
use_cases.description: CRO specialists use heatmaps and funnel analysis to identify where visitors drop off and prioritize which page elements to test.
use_cases.audience: Conversion rate optimization and growth teams

use_cases.title: UX research and usability testing
use_cases.description: Product and UX teams replay session recordings and recruit real users through Hotjar's panel to diagnose usability problems.

use_cases.title: Customer feedback collection
use_cases.description: Marketing and support teams deploy on-site surveys and feedback widgets to capture qualitative visitor sentiment at scale.

enrichment.short_description: Hotjar Surveys lets teams build no-code, responsive on-site or email surveys to collect direct feedback from website visitors.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (20 responses/month); paid plans from approximately EUR 48/month
enrichment.seo_title: Hotjar Surveys Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Hotjar Surveys is a no-code survey tool for collecting on-site visitor feedback. See 2026 pricing, features, pros, cons, and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Hotjar Surveys is the survey and feedback-collection feature within Hotjar's Ask product line, built by Hotjar, a behavior analytics company founded in 2014 in Malta by David Darmanin and co-founders including Erik Naslund, Johan Malmberg, Marc von Brockdorff, and Jonathan Vella. Hotjar operated as a fully remote, self-funded startup for years before being acquired by digital experience analytics company Contentsquare in September 2021, after which it continued operating under its own brand within a combined organization of more than 1,000 employees. Hotjar Surveys lets marketing, product, and UX teams build fully responsive surveys using a no-code editor, then distribute them either as an on-site widget triggered by page rules and visitor behavior, as a standalone shareable link, or via email, making it flexible enough to gather feedback both in-context on a live website and out-of-context from an existing customer list. The product handles response storage permanently on paid plans, includes logic for skipping or branching questions based on prior answers, and is built with GDPR and CCPA compliance in mind, including anonymization and data-retention controls appropriate for European and North American privacy regulations. According to Hotjar's own reporting, the surveys product has collected feedback from more than 402 million respondents across over 180 countries, and Hotjar as a whole, including Surveys, is used on more than 1.3 million websites by brands such as Nintendo, Adobe, T-Mobile, and HubSpot, with tens of thousands of new signups occurring monthly. Hotjar Surveys operates on a freemium model requiring no credit card to start: the free Basic tier allows up to 20 survey responses per month, sufficient for early-stage testing or low-traffic sites, while paid tiers unlock unlimited surveys and higher response caps. Historically, Ask's paid tiers were priced separately from Observe (Hotjar's heatmaps and recordings product) at roughly EUR 48 per month for Plus (250 responses), EUR 64 per month for Business (500 responses), and EUR 128 per month for Scale (higher volumes with more advanced feedback tooling); Hotjar is migrating self-serve customers throughout 2026 onto a unified Contentsquare pricing structure with Growth, Pro, and Enterprise tiers. Hotjar Surveys is most useful for growth and product teams that want direct, qualitative context to pair with quantitative behavior data from heatmaps and session recordings, letting them ask visitors why they behaved a certain way rather than only observing what they did. Its closest competitors include dedicated survey tools like Typeform, SurveyMonkey, and Qualaroo, as well as feedback features built into other analytics platforms such as FullStory and Mouseflow; Hotjar Surveys differentiates by being tightly integrated with Hotjar's broader behavior analytics suite, allowing teams to trigger surveys based on the same visitor session data used for heatmaps and recordings, and by its very large respondent network drawn from Hotjar's installed base across 1.3 million websites, though it lacks some of the advanced branching logic and dedicated research-panel tooling found in standalone survey specialists.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Hotjar Surveys is the feedback-collection component of Hotjar's Ask product suite, letting teams build no-code surveys and deploy them directly on their website, via link, or through email. It is built and maintained by Hotjar, the Malta-founded behavior analytics company acquired by Contentsquare in 2021.

The tool is designed to sit alongside Hotjar's heatmaps and session recordings so teams can pair quantitative behavior data with qualitative visitor feedback, answering not just what users did on a page but why they did it.

Hotjar reports that its surveys have collected responses from more than 402 million respondents across over 180 countries, reflecting broad adoption across the more than 1.3 million websites that use Hotjar's products.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Surveys can be built with a drag-and-drop, no-code editor and deployed as an on-site widget triggered by rules like time on page, scroll depth, or exit intent, as a standalone link, or sent via email to an existing customer or subscriber list.
The product supports permanent response storage on paid plans, question branching and skip logic, and responsive design that adapts survey layout across desktop and mobile visitors.

Privacy and compliance features include GDPR and CCPA-aligned data handling, and because Surveys is part of the broader Hotjar platform, response data can be viewed alongside session recordings and heatmap data for the same visitors where available.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Hotjar Surveys is available on a free Basic tier that allows up to 20 responses per month with no credit card required, making it accessible for testing or low-traffic sites. Paid tiers unlock unlimited surveys and higher monthly response volumes.

Historically, paid Ask tiers were priced separately from Hotjar's Observe product at roughly EUR 48 per month for Plus (250 responses), EUR 64 per month for Business (500 responses), and EUR 128 per month for Scale. As part of the broader Contentsquare integration, Hotjar is migrating self-serve customers to a unified pricing structure through 2026, so current published pricing may differ by account.

features.title: No-code survey builder

features.description: Ability to share surveys as a standalone link or send them via email outside the website context.

features.title: Question branching and skip logic
features.description: Conditional logic that adapts follow-up questions based on a respondent's previous answers.

features.title: Permanent response storage
features.description: Survey responses are stored permanently on paid plans for long-term analysis and reporting.

features.title: GDPR and CCPA-aligned privacy controls
features.description: Data handling built to align with major privacy regulations for collecting visitor feedback responsibly.

features.title: Cross-device responsive design
features.description: Surveys automatically adapt their layout for desktop, tablet, and mobile respondents.

features.title: Integration with Hotjar behavior data
features.description: Survey responses can be reviewed alongside heatmaps and session recordings for the same visitors.

pros: Free tier with no credit card required, offering up to 20 responses per month
Deep integration with Hotjar's heatmaps and session recordings for combined quantitative and qualitative insight
No-code builder makes it accessible to marketing and product teams without developer support
Flexible distribution via on-site widget, link, or email
Large existing respondent network drawn from Hotjar's 1.3 million-plus website install base
cons: Free tier's 20-response monthly cap is limiting for anything beyond small-scale testing
Historically priced as a separate subscription from Hotjar's Observe product, adding cost complexity
Less advanced branching and logic than dedicated survey specialists like Typeform
Support is largely ticket-based with multi-day response times
Pricing is in transition as Hotjar migrates to unified Contentsquare plans, creating some uncertainty for new buyers

faqs.question: Is Hotjar Surveys free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, Hotjar Surveys offers a free Basic tier with up to 20 responses per month and no credit card required. Paid plans unlock unlimited surveys and higher response volumes.

faqs.question: How is Hotjar Surveys different from Hotjar heatmaps?
faqs.answer: Hotjar Surveys is part of Hotjar's Ask product and focuses on collecting direct, qualitative visitor feedback, while heatmaps and session recordings are part of the Observe product and focus on visualizing quantitative behavior.

faqs.question: Can Hotjar Surveys be sent via email?
faqs.answer: Yes, surveys can be distributed as an on-site widget, a standalone shareable link, or sent directly via email.

faqs.question: How much does Hotjar Surveys cost on paid plans?
faqs.answer: Paid Ask tiers have historically started around EUR 48 per month for 250 responses, scaling up to EUR 128 per month for higher volumes, though pricing is migrating to a unified Contentsquare structure in 2026.

faqs.question: Does Hotjar Surveys support branching logic?
faqs.answer: Yes, surveys support question branching and skip logic based on a respondent's previous answers.

faqs.question: Who makes Hotjar Surveys?
faqs.answer: Hotjar Surveys is built by Hotjar, founded in Malta in 2014 and acquired by Contentsquare in 2021.

use_cases.description: Growth teams pair survey responses with heatmap and session recording data from the same visitors to understand both what happened and why.

enrichment.short_description: Housecall Pro is field service management software for home service businesses covering scheduling, invoicing, and payments.
enrichment.seo_title: Housecall Pro Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Housecall Pro is field service software for scheduling, invoicing, and payments. See 2026 pricing, features, pros, cons, and top alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Housecall Pro is a cloud-based field service management platform founded in 2013 in San Diego, California, by Ian Heidt, Roland Ligtenberg, Reza Olfat, Adam Perry-Pelletier, and Chris Zwickilton. The founding idea traced back to Ian Heidt's father, a house painter, and the co-founders' observation that busy home service professionals lacked easy-to-use software to manage scheduling, invoicing, and customer communication from the field. The company has since relocated its primary headquarters to Denver, Colorado, and grown to roughly 1,000 to 1,700 employees serving more than 200,000 home service professionals across trades including HVAC, plumbing, electrical, cleaning, landscaping, and pest control. Housecall Pro has raised more than $174 million in total funding from investors including Permira, Permira Growth Opportunities, Vista Credit Partners, Delta-v Capital, Invus Opportunities, NewView Capital, and Akkadian Ventures, including a $125 million round from Permira Growth Opportunities and Vista Credit Partners to fuel continued product and market expansion. Technically, the platform combines a web-based back office with free native iOS and Android mobile apps used by field technicians, enabling real-time synchronization of job schedules, customer records, and payment status between the office and the field. The core feature set covers online booking that lets customers self-schedule appointments 24/7 through Google or a business's website, automated invoicing with payment reminders and in-app card processing, drag-and-drop scheduling and dispatching with a visual job board, text- or email-based quotes and proposals, and integrated review management for collecting and showcasing customer feedback. Higher tiers add route optimization with real-time traffic-based reordering, flat-rate pricing books, photo reports with annotation tools, employee GPS tracking, technician commission tracking, two-way QuickBooks Online sync, a sales proposal tool for upselling, recurring service plan management, open API access for custom integrations, and dedicated onboarding specialists. Housecall Pro is sold in three main pricing tiers: Basic at $59 per month billed annually (or $79 per month billed monthly) for a single user, covering online booking, invoicing and payments, scheduling and dispatching, quotes, and review management; Essentials at $149 per month annually (or $189 monthly) for up to five users, adding routes, flat-rate pricing, checklist automations, photo reports, GPS tracking, commissions, and QuickBooks sync; and Max at $299 per month annually (or $329 monthly) for up to eight users, with additional users priced at $35 per month each, adding the sales proposal tool, recurring service plans, real-time route optimization, open API access, and escalated phone support with a dedicated onboarding specialist. All plans include live chat and phone support, free mobile apps, card processing around 2.59 percent per transaction, Spanish-language support, access to business and consumer financing options, and offline mobile functionality. Housecall Pro's target audience is small to mid-sized home service contractors and trade businesses looking to digitize scheduling, billing, and customer communication in one platform. It competes most directly with ServiceTitan, which targets larger enterprise field service operations, as well as Jobber, FieldPulse, Workiz, and Thryv; Housecall Pro differentiates through its combination of affordability relative to ServiceTitan, an easy-to-learn interface, strong customer support reputation including an active user community on platforms like Facebook, and deep invoicing and payments functionality, though independent reviews note the mobile app can be glitchy, reporting and data export are less robust than desired, the web interface can feel dated, and pricing can be steep for the smallest one- or two-person operations.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Housecall Pro is a field service management platform built for home service businesses such as HVAC, plumbing, electrical, cleaning, and landscaping companies. It was founded in 2013 in San Diego, California, by Ian Heidt, Roland Ligtenberg, Reza Olfat, Adam Perry-Pelletier, and Chris Zwickilton, and today operates primarily out of Denver, Colorado, with over 200,000 home service professionals on the platform.

The company has raised more than $174 million in funding from investors including Permira and Vista Credit Partners, backing continued expansion of its scheduling, payments, and marketing tools for trade businesses.

Housecall Pro pairs a web-based office dashboard with free mobile apps for field technicians, letting office staff and crews stay synchronized on job status, customer details, and payments in real time from any location.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The platform's core workflow covers 24/7 online booking, drag-and-drop scheduling and dispatching, text- and email-based quotes, automated invoicing with payment reminders, and integrated card processing so technicians can collect payment on-site.

Higher-tier plans add route optimization, flat-rate pricing books, photo reports with annotation, employee GPS tracking, commission tracking for technicians, two-way QuickBooks Online sync, recurring service plan management, and an open API for custom integrations.

Review management tools help businesses collect and showcase customer testimonials, while a built-in sales proposal tool on the top tier helps technicians upsell additional services directly from the field.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Housecall Pro is sold in three tiers priced per month with annual billing offering a discount over monthly billing. Basic starts at $59 per month annually for one user, Essentials at $149 per month annually for up to five users, and Max at $299 per month annually for up to eight users, with additional users on Max priced at $35 per month each.

Each tier is cumulative, meaning Essentials includes everything in Basic and Max includes everything in Essentials, with feature access expanding alongside user count. All plans include live chat and phone support, free mobile apps, and card processing at roughly 2.59 percent per transaction.

features.description: API access on the Max plan for building custom integrations with other business systems.

Strong customer support reputation, including an active user community
Easy-to-use drag-and-drop scheduling and built-in phone system
Free mobile apps for iOS and Android used by field technicians
Scales from solo operators to larger teams across three pricing tiers
cons: Pricing can be steep for very small, one- or two-person operations
Mobile app can be glitchy at times according to user reviews
Reporting and data export features are less robust than some competitors
Web interface can feel dated and clunky compared to newer field service tools
Mobile app functionality is limited when offline, restricting field use in low-connectivity areas

faqs.question: Who founded Housecall Pro?
faqs.answer: Housecall Pro was founded in 2013 in San Diego, California, by Ian Heidt, Roland Ligtenberg, Reza Olfat, Adam Perry-Pelletier, and Chris Zwickilton.

faqs.question: Does Housecall Pro have a mobile app?
faqs.answer: Yes, Housecall Pro offers free native iOS and Android apps for field technicians that sync in real time with the web-based office dashboard.

faqs.question: What trades use Housecall Pro?
faqs.answer: Housecall Pro is used by home service businesses including HVAC, plumbing, electrical, cleaning, landscaping, and pest control companies.

faqs.question: Does Housecall Pro integrate with QuickBooks?
faqs.answer: Yes, Housecall Pro offers two-way synchronization with QuickBooks Online starting on its Essentials plan.

faqs.question: How many users are included in each Housecall Pro plan?
faqs.answer: Basic includes 1 user, Essentials includes up to 5 users, and Max includes up to 8 users, with additional users on Max available for $35 per month each.
