enrichment.short_description: HTMLy is a free, open-source, databaseless PHP blogging platform and flat-file CMS that requires no database to run.
enrichment.seo_title: HTMLy Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: HTMLy is a free, open-source, databaseless PHP blogging platform. See 2026 features, pricing, pros, cons, and alternatives for flat-file CMS users.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: HTMLy is an open-source, databaseless blogging platform and flat-file content management system built in PHP, created and maintained by a developer known as DanPros, with source code hosted publicly at github.com/danpros/htmly. Rather than storing posts in a MySQL or other relational database, HTMLy stores every article as a plain text file on the server's file system using Markdown-like formatting, then uses a custom indexing algorithm to organize and retrieve content by date, category, tag, or author. The project's core selling point is architectural simplicity: because there is no database layer to install, configure, back up, or secure, HTMLy can run on extremely modest infrastructure, including shared hosting plans with as little as 512 MB of RAM, while the project states its indexing approach remains fast even with more than 20,000 posts and hundreds of tags. HTMLy is licensed under the GNU General Public License version 2 or later, making it fully free to download, modify, and redistribute, with no company, funding history, or commercial entity behind it beyond its open-source maintainer and contributor community; as a result, there is no confirmed public founding year, headquarters, or company size associated with the project, distinguishing it from venture-backed blogging SaaS products. System requirements are minimal: PHP 7.2 or higher along with the Mbstring, XML, Intl, and GD extensions, plus the ZIP extension if administrators want to use HTMLy's built-in backup functionality. Feature-wise, HTMLy supports archives, categories, tags, and automatic XML sitemap generation for SEO, full-text search across the entire post library, two-factor authentication for the admin login, scheduled publishing for posts written in advance, custom fields for extending post metadata, a menu editor with one-click updates, and built-in tracking of popular posts. Installation can be done either by downloading the source from GitHub directly or by using HTMLy's online installer script, which pulls the latest release automatically, making setup accessible even to users without command-line experience. HTMLy's target audience is developers, technical bloggers, and website owners who want a lightweight, fast, and low-maintenance publishing platform without the overhead, attack surface, and hosting requirements of a database-driven CMS like WordPress; it is particularly popular among users running personal tech blogs, documentation sites, and portfolio blogs on cheap or free shared hosting where a full LAMP-stack CMS would be unnecessarily heavy. It competes with other flat-file and static-adjacent publishing tools such as Grav, Kirby, Jekyll, Hugo, and Ghost, as well as, at a much larger scale, WordPress; HTMLy differentiates by staying true to a pure PHP, no-build-step, no-database model that non-technical users can still deploy on ordinary shared hosting without a static site generator's command-line build process, while sacrificing some of the plugin ecosystem, theme marketplace, and multi-author collaboration tooling that database-backed platforms like WordPress and Ghost offer at scale.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: HTMLy is a free, open-source blogging platform that stores every post as a flat text file rather than relying on a database, giving it a lightweight footprint that runs comfortably on inexpensive shared hosting. It is built in PHP by a developer known as DanPros and distributed under the GPL v2 or later license through a public GitHub repository.

The platform's databaseless design is its defining characteristic: there is no MySQL or PostgreSQL instance to install, configure, or secure, which simplifies both setup and ongoing maintenance for site owners who don't want to manage a separate database server.

Because there is no company or funding history behind HTMLy, it operates purely as a community-maintained open-source project, with development activity, releases, and support happening through GitHub issues and the project's own site rather than a commercial support organization.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: HTMLy supports the core features expected of a blogging platform, including archives, categories, tags, automatic sitemap generation, and full-text search, all built on top of a custom file-indexing algorithm designed to stay fast even as a site grows to tens of thousands of posts.

Security and workflow features include two-factor authentication for the admin panel, scheduled post publishing, custom fields for extending content metadata, and a menu editor that updates site navigation with a single click.

Because HTMLy requires no database, backups are as simple as copying the site's files, and the platform includes built-in backup functionality via the PHP ZIP extension, further reducing the operational overhead compared to database-driven CMS platforms.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: HTMLy is entirely free and open source under the GPL v2 or later license, with no paid tiers, subscriptions, or premium feature gates. Anyone can download the source code from GitHub or use the online installer at no cost.

The only ongoing expense is web hosting, and because HTMLy has minimal server requirements, running only PHP 7.2 or higher without a database, it can operate on very inexpensive shared hosting plans or even free hosting tiers that support basic PHP, making the total cost of ownership close to zero for most users.

features.title: Databaseless flat-file storage
features.description: Posts are stored as plain text files rather than in a database, simplifying hosting and backups.

features.title: Archives, categories, and tags
features.description: Standard content organization tools for browsing posts by date, category, tag, or author.

features.title: Automatic sitemap generation
features.description: Built-in XML sitemap creation to support search engine indexing without extra plugins.

features.title: Full-text search
features.description: Search across the entire post library using HTMLy's custom file-indexing algorithm.

features.title: Two-factor authentication
features.description: Optional 2FA for the admin login to add a layer of account security.

features.title: Scheduled publishing
features.description: Ability to write posts in advance and have them publish automatically at a set date and time.

features.title: Custom fields
features.description: Extendable post metadata fields for adding structured information beyond the default content model.

features.title: One-click menu editor
features.description: A simple interface for updating site navigation without editing template files directly.

pros: Completely free and open source under the GPL license
No database required, which simplifies hosting, backups, and security
Runs well on minimal shared hosting resources, even with 512 MB of RAM
Remains fast even with tens of thousands of posts according to the project
Simple installation via an online installer script for non-technical users
cons: Smaller plugin and theme ecosystem compared to WordPress
No official commercial support, funding, or company backing the project
Limited multi-author collaboration tooling compared to larger CMS platforms
Founding year, team size, and roadmap are not publicly documented
Requires PHP hosting, so it is not usable on purely static hosting like some flat-file alternatives

faqs.question: Is HTMLy free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, HTMLy is completely free and open source under the GNU General Public License version 2 or later.

faqs.question: Does HTMLy require a database?
faqs.answer: No, HTMLy is a databaseless, flat-file CMS that stores posts as plain text files on the file system instead of in a database.

faqs.question: What are HTMLy's server requirements?
faqs.answer: HTMLy requires PHP 7.2 or greater along with the Mbstring, XML, Intl, and GD extensions, plus the ZIP extension for backup functionality.

faqs.question: Who created HTMLy?
faqs.answer: HTMLy was created by a developer known as DanPros and is maintained as an open-source project on GitHub.

faqs.question: How many posts can HTMLy handle?
faqs.answer: The project states HTMLy's indexing algorithm remains fast even with more than 20,000 posts and hundreds of tags.

faqs.question: How do I install HTMLy?
faqs.answer: HTMLy can be installed by downloading the source code from GitHub or by using its online installer script, which does not require command-line access.

use_cases.title: Lightweight personal blogging
use_cases.description: Individual bloggers can run a fast, low-maintenance blog on inexpensive shared hosting without managing a database.

use_cases.title: Low-resource hosting environments
use_cases.description: Site owners with limited server resources, such as free or budget shared hosting, can run HTMLy where a database-driven CMS would be too heavy.

use_cases.title: Developer-friendly documentation sites
use_cases.description: Developers can use HTMLy's flat-file structure to version-control content alongside code and deploy simple documentation or portfolio blogs.

features: Full source code under GPL v2+ license

enrichment.short_description: HTTPie is a user-friendly HTTP client and API testing tool available as an open-source CLI and a desktop and web app.
enrichment.pricing_model: Freemium (Open Source CLI, plus paid team and enterprise features)
enrichment.seo_title: HTTPie Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: HTTPie is a user-friendly API testing client with a free CLI and desktop app. See 2026 pricing, features, pros, cons, and top alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: HTTPie began in 2012 as an open-source command-line HTTP client created by developer Jakub Roztocil, built as a more human-friendly alternative to curl for interacting with web APIs from the terminal. Written in Python and using the Requests and Pygments libraries under the hood, the original httpie command popularized colorized, readable JSON output, intuitive syntax for setting headers and request bodies, and sensible defaults, which drove it to become one of the most-starred command-line tools on GitHub, with the core httpie/httpie repository amassing tens of thousands of stars. Roztocil later formalized the open-source project into a company, HTTPie Inc., headquartered in San Francisco, California, with Roztocil continuing as Founder and CEO. In 2020, the company raised a 6.5 million dollar seed funding round led by Coatue, with participation from Blossom Capital and angel investors who were founders of Intercom, Checkout.com, and Monzo, funding the expansion from a single CLI tool into a broader API development platform. That platform now includes HTTPie Desktop and HTTPie for Web, a cross-platform GUI application available for macOS (both Intel and Apple Silicon), Windows, and Linux, as well as a browser-based version, both of which layer a visual interface, request history, collections, environment variables, and team collaboration features on top of the same request engine that powers the CLI. More recently, the company has added HTTPie AI, an AI assistant aimed at helping developers generate, debug, and document API requests throughout the API lifecycle. Architecturally, HTTPie's product line spans three surfaces: the original open-source terminal client, installable via package managers like apt, brew, and pip; the newer desktop and web GUI, which was in public beta as of the company's most recent releases; and cloud-connected team features for sharing collections and environments across an organization. The terminal client remains fully free and open source with no paid tier, reflecting its role as both the company's original product and its primary developer-acquisition channel, while the desktop and web app is free for individual use and reserves collaborative, team-oriented functionality for paid plans; HTTPie does not publish detailed self-serve pricing for its team and enterprise tiers, instead directing prospective business customers to contact sales for custom quotes, a common pattern among open-source-rooted developer tools monetizing through a top-of-funnel free product. HTTPie's target audience spans individual developers and hobbyists who rely on the free CLI or desktop app for everyday API testing, up through engineering teams and enterprises that need shared collections, environment management, and collaboration features for testing REST, GraphQL, and general HTTP APIs together. Its most direct competitors are Postman, the dominant player in the API client space, along with Insomnia, Thunder Client, Bruno, and the open-source Hoppscotch; HTTPie differentiates through its command-line-first heritage and reputation for simplicity and readability, its fully open-source terminal client with no feature gating, and a lighter-weight, more developer-centric desktop experience relative to Postman's broader but heavier feature set, though it has a smaller collaboration and enterprise feature set than Postman given Postman's longer head start monetizing team workflows.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: HTTPie started in 2012 as an open-source command-line HTTP client created by Jakub Roztocil, designed to make testing APIs from the terminal more intuitive than raw curl commands through colorized output and simpler syntax. The project's popularity on GitHub eventually led Roztocil to found HTTPie Inc., headquartered in San Francisco, and raise a 6.5 million dollar seed round led by Coatue in 2020 to expand beyond the CLI.

Today HTTPie spans three connected products: the original free, open-source terminal client; HTTPie Desktop and Web, a cross-platform GUI application in public beta for macOS, Windows, and Linux plus a browser version; and HTTPie AI, an AI assistant for generating and debugging API requests.

The company's strategy mirrors many open-source-rooted developer tools: keep the original CLI product fully free and open to drive adoption and brand loyalty among individual developers, then monetize collaborative, team-oriented features in the newer desktop and web products.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The HTTPie CLI offers human-friendly syntax for constructing HTTP requests, automatic JSON formatting and syntax highlighting, built-in support for sessions, authentication, file uploads, and downloads, and plugin support for extending functionality, all installable via common package managers like brew, apt, and pip.

HTTPie Desktop and Web add a visual request builder, response viewer, request history, saved collections, and environment variables for managing different API configurations such as staging versus production, bringing a Postman-like GUI experience to the same underlying request engine used by the CLI.

HTTPie AI layers generative AI assistance on top of the API testing workflow, helping developers draft requests, interpret responses, and troubleshoot errors, reflecting the company's broader push to support the full API development lifecycle rather than just request execution.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The HTTPie command-line client is completely free and open source with no paid tier or feature restrictions, and it will remain so given its role as the project's original open-source foundation. The HTTPie Desktop and Web app is also free for individual use and does not require creating an account for basic usage.

Team and collaboration features, such as shared collections and workspace management, are positioned as paid capabilities within the desktop and web app, but HTTPie does not publish detailed self-serve pricing for these tiers; prospective team and enterprise customers are directed to contact HTTPie's sales team for a custom quote.

features.title: Human-friendly CLI syntax
features.description: Intuitive command-line syntax for building HTTP requests, simpler to read and write than raw curl commands.

features.title: Colorized, formatted output
features.description: Automatic JSON formatting and syntax highlighting in the terminal for easier response inspection.

features.title: Cross-platform desktop and web app
features.description: A GUI client available for macOS, Windows, Linux, and the browser, layering a visual interface over the same request engine as the CLI.

features.title: Request collections and history
features.description: Saved collections and request history for organizing and reusing API calls across projects.

features.title: Environment variables
features.description: Configurable environments for managing different variable sets across staging, production, and other contexts.

features.title: Sessions and authentication support
features.description: Built-in support for persistent sessions, various authentication schemes, file uploads, and downloads.

features.title: Plugin ecosystem
features.description: Extensibility through plugins for adding custom functionality to the CLI client.

features.title: HTTPie AI assistant
features.description: An AI-powered assistant for generating, debugging, and documenting API requests throughout the testing workflow.

pros: Core command-line client is fully free, open source, and has no feature restrictions
Simpler, more readable syntax and output than raw curl for testing APIs
Cross-platform desktop and web app extends the same engine into a visual interface
Strong open-source reputation and large GitHub community
Backed by seed funding, supporting continued development of AI and collaboration features
cons: Desktop and web app was still in public beta as of its most recent releases
Team and enterprise pricing is not publicly published, requiring a sales conversation
Smaller collaboration and enterprise feature set compared to Postman
AI features are newer and less proven than the core request-testing functionality
Company size and detailed growth metrics are not publicly disclosed

faqs.question: Is HTTPie free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, the HTTPie command-line client is completely free and open source, and the HTTPie Desktop and Web app is also free for individual use.

faqs.question: Who created HTTPie?
faqs.answer: HTTPie was created in 2012 by Jakub Roztocil, who later founded HTTPie Inc., headquartered in San Francisco, and continues to serve as Founder and CEO.

faqs.question: Does HTTPie have a GUI app?
faqs.answer: Yes, HTTPie Desktop and Web is a cross-platform GUI application available for macOS, Windows, Linux, and the browser, in addition to the original command-line tool.

faqs.question: How much does HTTPie cost for teams?
faqs.answer: HTTPie does not publish self-serve team or enterprise pricing; businesses need to contact HTTPie's sales team for a custom quote.

faqs.question: What is HTTPie AI?
faqs.answer: HTTPie AI is an AI-powered assistant built into HTTPie's products to help developers generate, debug, and document API requests.

faqs.question: How is HTTPie different from Postman?
faqs.answer: HTTPie originated as a free, open-source command-line tool with simpler syntax and output, while Postman has historically focused on a GUI-first approach with a larger enterprise and collaboration feature set.

use_cases.title: Command-line API testing
use_cases.description: Developers use the free HTTPie CLI to quickly test and debug API endpoints directly from the terminal during development.

use_cases.title: Visual API exploration
use_cases.description: Developers who prefer a GUI can use HTTPie Desktop or Web to build requests, view responses, and manage collections visually.

use_cases.title: Team API collaboration
use_cases.description: Engineering teams use HTTPie's collections and environment variables to share and standardize API testing workflows across staging and production.

features: Cross-platform GUI for macOS, Windows, and Linux
